source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Direct application of the one-sample Z test.
question_text: A machine is calibrated to produce parts with mean length \(50\) mm. The population standard deviation is known to be \(\sigma=8\) mm. A sample of \(n=64\) parts has \(\bar x=52.1\). Test \(H_0:\mu=50\) against \(H_1:\mu\ne50\) at level \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: Use the known-variance statistic \[Z=\frac{\bar X-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}.\] Here \(Z=(52.1-50)/(8/\sqrt{64})=2.10\). For a two-sided level-5% test, reject if \(|Z|>z_{0.025}=1.96\). Since \(2.10>1.96\), reject \(H_0\). The two-sided P-value is \(2P(Z\ge2.10)\approx0.0358\), also below 0.05.
thought_text: Known population standard deviation means the denominator is (sigma/sqrt n), not (s/sqrt n).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Right-tailed P-value practice.
question_text: A chemical process has known \(\sigma=1.5\). From \(n=36\) runs, \(\bar x=10.32\). Test \(H_0:\mu=10.0\) against \(H_1:\mu>10.0\). Report the P-value and the decision at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: The statistic is \[Z=\frac{10.32-10.0}{1.5/\sqrt{36}}=\frac{0.32}{0.25}=1.28.\] For \(H_1:\mu>10\), the P-value is the upper-tail probability \(P(Z\ge1.28)\approx0.1003\). Since \(0.1003>0.05\), do not reject \(H_0\).
thought_text: Do not double the P-value for a one-sided test.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Direct application of the one-sample T test.
question_text: A normal population is sampled with \(n=16\), \(\bar x=101.8\), and \(s=3.2\). Test \(H_0:\mu=100\) against \(H_1:\mu\ne100\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: Since \(\sigma\) is unknown and the population is normal, use \[T=\frac{\bar X-\mu_0}{S/\sqrt n}\sim t_{n-1}\quad\text{under }H_0.\] The observed value is \(t=(101.8-100)/(3.2/\sqrt{16})=1.8/0.8=2.25\), with \(df=15\). The critical value is \(t_{0.025,15}\approx2.131\). Since \(|2.25|>2.131\), reject \(H_0\). The P-value is about \(0.040\).
thought_text: If (s) is used in a small-sample mean test, the reference distribution is T under the normal model.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. One-sided T-test practice.
question_text: A battery vendor claims the mean lifetime is \(78\) hours. In a normal sample of \(n=12\), \(\bar x=74.2\) and \(s=5.1\). Test \(H_0:\mu=78\) against \(H_1:\mu<78\). Give the conclusion at 5% and at 1%.
solution_text: The test statistic is \[t=\frac{74.2-78}{5.1/\sqrt{12}}\approx\frac{-3.8}{1.472}=-2.58,\qquad df=11.\] The left-tailed P-value is approximately \(0.013\). Therefore reject at the 5% level, but do not reject at the 1% level.
thought_text: A result can be significant at 5% but not at 1%; significance is not an all-or-nothing property independent of (alpha).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Direct application of the variance test.
question_text: A normal process is acceptable only if its variance is no more than \(9\). From \(n=21\) observations, \(s^2=14.4\). Test \(H_0:\sigma^2=9\) against \(H_1:\sigma^2>9\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(\chi^2_{0.05,20}=31.41\), where the subscript denotes right-tail area.
solution_text: For a normal sample, \[\chi^2=\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2}\sim\chi^2_{n-1}\quad\text{under }H_0.\] The observed statistic is \(\chi^2=20(14.4)/9=32.0\), with \(df=20\). For \(H_1:\sigma^2>9\), reject for large values. Since \(32.0>31.41\), reject \(H_0\). The P-value is about \(0.04\).
thought_text: Large sample variance gives a large chi-squared statistic, so right-tailed alternatives reject in the upper tail.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Standard-deviation null phrased as variance test.
question_text: A normal measurement system is advertised to have standard deviation \(\sigma=0.50\). A sample of \(n=16\) measurements gives \(s=0.72\). Test \(H_0:\sigma=0.50\) against \(H_1:\sigma\ne0.50\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(\chi^2_{0.025,15}=27.49\) and \(\chi^2_{0.975,15}=6.26\), in right-tail notation.
solution_text: Testing a standard deviation is done through the variance. Under \(H_0\), \(\sigma_0^2=0.25\), so \[\chi^2=\frac{15(0.72)^2}{0.25}=31.10,\qquad df=15.\] A two-sided 5% test rejects if \(\chi^2>27.49\) or \(\chi^227.49\), reject \(H_0\). The evidence suggests the standard deviation differs from, and in this sample exceeds, \(0.50\).
thought_text: The null may be written using (sigma), but the chi-squared statistic uses (sigma_0^2).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Assumption checklist.
question_text: For each test, state the key population assumption for the exact small-sample version: (i) Z test for \(\mu\) with known \(\sigma\); (ii) T test for \(\mu\) with unknown \(\sigma\); (iii) chi-squared test for \(\sigma^2\).
solution_text: (i) Exact Z testing assumes a normal population when \(n\) is small; for large \(n\), the CLT can justify an approximate Z test. (ii) The exact one-sample T test assumes a normal population. Large samples are more forgiving for testing a mean, but the exact theorem is normal. (iii) The chi-squared variance test requires a normal population. This test is sensitive to non-normality.
thought_text: Variance tests based on ((n-1)S^2/sigma^2) are usually the least robust to non-normal data.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Conceptual P-value interpretation.
question_text: A test of \(H_0:\mu=20\) against \(H_1:\mu>20\) gives P-value \(0.032\). Which interpretation is correct? Explain briefly. There is a 3.2% probability that \(H_0\) is true. If \(H_0\) were true, the probability of a result at least as favorable to \(H_1\) as the observed one is 3.2%. The probability of a Type I error is 3.2%.
solution_text: The correct interpretation is (b). A P-value is computed under the assumption that \(H_0\) is true and measures tail extremeness in the direction of the alternative. Statement (a) treats the hypothesis as random without a Bayesian model. Statement (c) confuses the observed P-value with the pre-chosen significance level \(\alpha\).
thought_text: The Type I error rate is controlled by the rejection rule before seeing the data; the P-value is computed after seeing the data.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Critical-value decision without P-value.
question_text: For a known-variance Z test of \(H_0:\mu=30\) against \(H_1:\mu>30\), the observed statistic is \(z=2.05\). At \(\alpha=0.01\), should \(H_0\) be rejected?
solution_text: For a right-tailed Z test at \(\alpha=0.01\), the critical value is \(z_{0.01}=2.326\). The rejection rule is \(Z>2.326\). Since \(2.05<2.326\), do not reject \(H_0\) at the 1% level.
thought_text: A statistic that would reject at 5% may fail to reject at 1%.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Sign of the rejection region.
question_text: A one-sample T test has \(df=8\), observed \(t=-2.00\), and alternative \(H_1:\mu<\mu_0\). At \(\alpha=0.05\), use \(t_{0.05,8}=1.860\) to make the decision.
solution_text: Because the alternative is left-tailed, the rejection rule is \(T<-t_{0.05,8}=-1.860\). The observed value \(-2.00<-1.860\), so reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level.
thought_text: The table usually reports positive upper-tail cutoffs. For a left-tailed test, put the minus sign on the cutoff.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Link between two-sided tests and intervals.
question_text: A 95% confidence interval for \(\mu\), computed using the same assumptions as a two-sided one-sample test, is \([12.4,15.7]\). Without recomputing the statistic, state whether a level-5% two-sided test rejects \(H_0:\mu=16\). What about \(H_0:\mu=14\)?
solution_text: For the matching two-sided test, reject exactly those null values outside the 95% confidence interval. The value \(16\) is outside \([12.4,15.7]\), so reject \(H_0:\mu=16\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). The value \(14\) lies inside the interval, so do not reject \(H_0:\mu=14\).
thought_text: This shortcut does not automatically apply to one-sided alternatives or mismatched confidence levels.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Basic test selection.
question_text: Choose the correct test statistic for each scenario: (i) known \(\sigma\), test for \(\mu\); (ii) unknown \(\sigma\), normal population, test for \(\mu\); (iii) normal population, test for \(\sigma^2\).
solution_text: (i) Use \(Z=(\bar X-\mu_0)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\). (ii) Use \(T=(\bar X-\mu_0)/(S/\sqrt n)\), with \(df=n-1\). (iii) Use \(\chi^2=(n-1)S^2/\sigma_0^2\), with \(df=n-1\).
thought_text: Most Chapter 19 mistakes start before arithmetic: the wrong test statistic is selected.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Computation from raw observations.
question_text: The ten observations below are from a normal population: \(23.4,21.9,22.7,24.1,22.5,23.0,21.8,22.2,23.6,22.9\). Test \(H_0:\mu=22.0\) against \(H_1:\mu>22.0\) at \(\alpha=0.01\).
solution_text: From the data, \(n=10\), \(\bar x=22.81\), and \(s\approx0.746\). The statistic is \[t=\frac{22.81-22.0}{0.746/\sqrt{10}}\approx3.43,\qquad df=9.\] For a right-tailed level-1% test, \(t_{0.01,9}\approx2.821\). Since \(3.43>2.821\), reject \(H_0\). The P-value is below 0.01.
thought_text: Raw data questions often hide whether the denominator should use (s) or (s^2). For a mean test, use (s/sqrt n).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Power calculation for a right-tailed Z test.
question_text: A right-tailed known-variance test uses \(H_0:\mu=100\) versus \(H_1:\mu>100\), with \(\alpha=0.05\), known \(\sigma=12\), and \(n=36\). Find the rejection rule in terms of \(\bar X\), then approximate the power when the true mean is \(\mu=106\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(12/\sqrt{36}=2\). The level-5% right-tailed rule is \[Z=\frac{\bar X-100}{2}>1.645,\] so reject when \(\bar X>100+1.645(2)=103.29\). If \(\mu=106\), then \(\bar X\sim N(106,2^2)\). The power is \[P_{\mu=106}(\bar X>103.29)=P\left(Z>\frac{103.29-106}{2}\right)=P(Z>-1.355)\approx0.912.\]
thought_text: Power is computed under an alternative value, while the critical cutoff is chosen to control (alpha) under (H_0).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. One-sided Z-test design.
question_text: For a right-tailed Z test of \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) with known \(\sigma=10\), how large should \(n\) be to have significance level \(0.05\) and power at least \(0.90\) when \(\mu=\mu_0+3\)?
solution_text: For a one-sided Z test, the usual planning approximation is \[n\ge\left(\frac{(z_\alpha+z_\beta)\sigma}{\delta}\right)^2,\] where \(\delta=3\), \(\alpha=0.05\), and \(\beta=0.10\). Using \(z_{0.05}=1.645\) and \(z_{0.10}=1.282\), \[n\ge\left(\frac{(1.645+1.282)10}{3}\right)^2=95.2.\] Round up to \(n=96\).
thought_text: Sample size must be rounded up; rounding down would miss the requested power.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Approximate two-sided planning problem.
question_text: Using a normal approximation, estimate the sample size needed for a two-sided known-variance test at \(\alpha=0.05\) to have power \(0.80\) for detecting \(|\mu-\mu_0|=2\), when \(\sigma=6\).
solution_text: For a two-sided planning approximation, use \[n\ge\left(\frac{(z_{\alpha/2}+z_\beta)\sigma}{\delta}\right)^2.\] Here \(z_{0.025}=1.96\), \(z_{0.20}=0.842\), \(\sigma=6\), and \(\delta=2\). Thus \[n\ge\left(\frac{(1.96+0.842)6}{2}\right)^2=70.66.\] Round up to \(n=71\).
thought_text: Two-sided tests need a larger cutoff than one-sided tests at the same (alpha), so they usually need more observations for the same target power.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Lower-variance alternative.
question_text: A normal process is claimed to have variance \(4\). A sample of \(n=25\) gives \(s^2=2.7\). Test \(H_0:\sigma^2=4\) against \(H_1:\sigma^2<4\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(\chi^2_{0.95,24}=13.85\), in right-tail notation.
solution_text: The statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{24(2.7)}{4}=16.2,\qquad df=24.\] For \(H_1:\sigma^213.85\), do not reject \(H_0\).
thought_text: A smaller observed (s^2) is not automatically significant; it must be small enough relative to the chi-squared lower-tail cutoff.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Testing a standard-deviation requirement.
question_text: A supplier promises a normal production process has standard deviation at most \(0.35\). From \(n=31\), \(s=0.44\). Test \(H_0:\sigma=0.35\) against \(H_1:\sigma>0.35\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(\chi^2_{0.05,30}=43.77\).
solution_text: Use the variance form with \(\sigma_0^2=0.35^2=0.1225\): \[\chi^2=\frac{30(0.44)^2}{0.1225}=\frac{5.808}{0.1225}=47.41.\] The right-tailed 5% cutoff is \(43.77\). Since \(47.41>43.77\), reject \(H_0\). The data provide evidence that the standard deviation exceeds \(0.35\).
thought_text: For a greater-than standard deviation alternative, large (s) produces a large chi-squared statistic.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Error interpretation.
question_text: In a test of \(H_0:\mu=50\) against \(H_1:\mu>50\), describe a Type I error and a Type II error in words. If the test has \(\alpha=0.05\), what probability is controlled at 0.05?
solution_text: A Type I error is rejecting \(H_0\) when the true mean is actually \(50\) under the null model. A Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when the true mean is greater than \(50\). The significance level \(\alpha=0.05\) controls the probability of a Type I error, not the probability of a Type II error. The Type II error probability depends on the true alternative mean, sample size, variance, and rejection rule.
thought_text: Never say (alpha) is the probability that (H_0) is false; it is a conditional error probability under (H_0).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Comparing one P-value to several levels.
question_text: A two-sided test reports P-value \(0.032\). State the decision at \(\alpha=0.10\), \(0.05\), and \(0.01\).
solution_text: Reject \(H_0\) when \(\text{P-value}\le\alpha\). Since \(0.032\le0.10\) and \(0.032\le0.05\), reject at the 10% and 5% levels. Since \(0.032>0.01\), do not reject at the 1% level.
thought_text: A P-value is the smallest conventional significance level at which the observed result would begin to reject.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Direction-of-alternative trap.
question_text: A normal sample has \(n=20\), \(\bar x=4.8\), \(s=1.5\). Test \(H_0:\mu=5\) against \(H_1:\mu>5\). A student computes \(t=-0.596\) and says the P-value is about \(0.28\), so the result is close to significant. Diagnose the statement.
solution_text: The statistic is correct: \(t=(4.8-5)/(1.5/\sqrt{20})\approx-0.596\), with \(df=19\). But the P-value for \(H_1:\mu>5\) is an upper-tail probability: \[P(T_{19}\ge -0.596)\approx0.72,\] not \(0.28\). The observed mean is below the null mean, which is opposite the direction of the alternative. There is no evidence for \(\mu>5\).
thought_text: For a one-sided test, the sign of the statistic matters as much as its magnitude.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Assumption diagnosis.
question_text: A sample of \(n=18\) waiting times is strongly right-skewed. A classmate applies the chi-squared test for \(H_0:\sigma^2=4\) and says it is exact because the statistic has \(17\) degrees of freedom. Diagnose the claim.
solution_text: The claim is incomplete and misleading. The degrees of freedom would be \(n-1=17\) only if the chi-squared theorem applies, but \((n-1)S^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-1}\) is an exact result for normal populations. Strongly right-skewed waiting times violate the normality condition, so the chi-squared variance test is not justified as exact. A transformed model, a model suited to waiting times, or a resampling method may be more appropriate depending on course rules.
thought_text: For means, large samples can soften non-normality concerns; for chi-squared variance tests, non-normality remains a serious issue.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Method-selection trap.
question_text: A problem states that a normal population has known \(\sigma=4\). A sample of \(n=25\) has \(\bar x=18.9\) and sample standard deviation \(s=5.1\). Test \(H_0:\mu=20\) against \(H_1:\mu<20\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Which standard deviation belongs in the test statistic?
solution_text: Use the known population standard deviation \(\sigma=4\), not the sample standard deviation \(s=5.1\). The statistic is \[z=\frac{18.9-20}{4/\sqrt{25}}=\frac{-1.1}{0.8}=-1.375.\] For a left-tailed 5% Z test, reject if \(z-1.645\), do not reject \(H_0\).
thought_text: If the problem says (sigma) is known, an extra reported (s) may be a distractor.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Paired-difference one-sample test.
question_text: Eight subjects are measured before and after a treatment. Let \(D=\text{after}-\text{before}\). The differences have \(\bar d=2.4\), \(s_d=3.0\), and are approximately normal. Test whether the mean change is positive: \(H_0:\mu_D=0\) versus \(H_1:\mu_D>0\), at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: This is a one-sample T test on the differences. The statistic is \[t=\frac{2.4-0}{3.0/\sqrt8}=2.26,\qquad df=7.\] For a right-tailed 5% test, \(t_{0.05,7}\approx1.895\). Since \(2.26>1.895\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence of a positive mean change.
thought_text: Paired data are not a two-sample problem after taking differences; Chapter 19 tools apply to the single sample (D_1,ldots,D_n).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Deriving the two-sided T-test rule.
question_text: For a normal sample with unknown \(\sigma\), derive the two-sided level-\(\alpha\) T-test rejection rule for \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) versus \(H_1:\mu\ne\mu_0\), and write the corresponding P-value formula.
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), \[T=\frac{\bar X-\mu_0}{S/\sqrt n}\sim t_{n-1}.\] A two-sided level-\(\alpha\) test puts \(\alpha/2\) probability in each tail, so the rejection rule is \[|T|>t_{\alpha/2,n-1}.\] If the observed value is \(t_{\text{obs}}\), then the P-value is \[2P(T_{n-1}\ge |t_{\text{obs}}|).\]
thought_text: The absolute value belongs in the two-sided rule because departures in either direction count as evidence.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Final-style diagnose-the-solution.
question_text: A student writes: "For \(n=9\), \(\bar x=68\), \(s=6\), test \(H_0:\mu=70\) versus \(H_1:\mu<70\). The statistic is \(z=(68-70)/(6/9)=-3\), so reject at 5%." Identify at least three errors and give the correct conclusion, assuming normality.
solution_text: Errors include: (1) \(\sigma\) is unknown, so use T, not Z. (2) The standard error is \(s/\sqrt n\), not \(s/n\). (3) The degrees of freedom are \(n-1=8\). A fourth issue is that the conclusion must use the left-tail T reference distribution. The correct statistic is \[t=\frac{68-70}{6/\sqrt9}=\frac{-2}{2}=-1.00,\qquad df=8.\] For a 5% left-tailed test, the cutoff is \(-t_{0.05,8}=-1.860\). Since \(-1.00>-1.860\), do not reject \(H_0\).
thought_text: Diagnose the method error before recomputing; otherwise a wrong denominator can create fake significance.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Final-style chi-squared error diagnosis.
question_text: A student tests \(H_0:\sigma^2=16\) versus \(H_1:\sigma^2\ne16\) from a normal sample with \(n=12\), \(s=5\). They compute \(\chi^2=12(25)/16=18.75\) and reject because it exceeds the right-tail 5% cutoff \(21.03\). Diagnose the errors and give the correct decision. Use \(\chi^2_{0.025,11}=21.92\) and \(\chi^2_{0.975,11}=3.82\).
solution_text: There are several errors. The numerator should be \((n-1)s^2\), not \(ns^2\), so the statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{11(25)}{16}=17.19,\qquad df=11.\] The alternative is two-sided, so a level-5% test uses both tails: reject if \(\chi^2>21.92\) or \(\chi^2<3.82\). Since \(17.19\) is between the two cutoffs, do not reject \(H_0\). The student's sentence also contradicts itself: \(18.75\) does not exceed \(21.03\).
thought_text: Two-sided variance tests have two rejection regions: one for unusually small (s^2) and one for unusually large (s^2).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Power as a function of the true mean.
question_text: A left-tailed known-variance Z test has \(H_0:\mu=40\), \(H_1:\mu<40\), \(\sigma=5\), \(n=25\), and \(\alpha=0.01\). Find the rejection threshold for \(\bar X\), then compute the power when \(\mu=37\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(5/\sqrt{25}=1\). For a left-tailed 1% Z test, reject when \[Z=\frac{\bar X-40}{1}<-z_{0.01}=-2.326,\] so reject when \(\bar X<37.674\). If \(\mu=37\), then \(\bar X\sim N(37,1^2)\). The power is \[P_{\mu=37}(\bar X<37.674)=P(Z<0.674)\approx0.75.\]
thought_text: For left-tailed tests, power increases as the true mean moves farther below (mu_0).

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Tail-area construction.
question_text: A known-variance Z test gives \(z_{\text{obs}}=-2.14\). Compute the P-value for each alternative: (i) \(H_1:\mu\mu_0\); (iii) \(H_1:\mu\ne\mu_0\).
thought_text: The same observed statistic can be strong evidence for one one-sided alternative and almost no evidence for the opposite one.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Final-style mixed selection.
question_text: For each scenario, choose the correct Chapter 19 test and write the statistic, but do not compute a numerical decision. \(n=40\), known \(\sigma\), test \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\). \(n=11\), normal population, unknown \(\sigma\), test \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\). \(n=30\), normal population, test \(H_0:\sigma=\sigma_0\). \(n=22\), strongly skewed population, test \(H_0:\sigma^2=\sigma_0^2\).
solution_text: (i) Use a Z test: \[Z=\frac{\bar X-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{40}}.\] (ii) Use a T test: \[T=\frac{\bar X-\mu_0}{S/\sqrt{11}},\qquad df=10.\] (iii) Use a chi-squared test on variance: \[\chi^2=\frac{29S^2}{\sigma_0^2},\qquad df=29.\] If the null is written with \(\sigma_0\), square it in the denominator. (iv) The exact chi-squared variance test is not justified because the population is strongly skewed. State that normality is missing or use another method if allowed.
thought_text: The test statistic is determined by the parameter being tested and whether the variance is known, not just by the sample size.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Final-style combined mean and variance problem.
question_text: A normal sample has \(n=25\), \(\bar x=47.6\), and \(s=4.8\). Conduct both tests: (a) \(H_0:\mu=50\) versus \(H_1:\mu\ne50\) at \(\alpha=0.01\); (b) \(H_0:\sigma^2=16\) versus \(H_1:\sigma^2>16\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(t_{0.005,24}=2.797\) and \(\chi^2_{0.05,24}=36.42\).
solution_text: (a) Since \(\sigma\) is unknown, use \[t=\frac{47.6-50}{4.8/\sqrt{25}}=\frac{-2.4}{0.96}=-2.50,\qquad df=24.\] For a two-sided 1% test, reject if \(|t|>2.797\). Since \(2.50(b) For the variance test, \[\chi^2=\frac{24(4.8)^2}{16}=34.56,\qquad df=24.\] The right-tailed 5% cutoff is \(36.42\). Since \(34.56<36.42\), do not reject \(H_0:\sigma^2=16\).
thought_text: The same dataset can support different tests, but each test has its own statistic, degrees of freedom, and tail direction.

source: exercises_ch19.html | Original. Multi-error final-style problem.
question_text: A student analyzes \(n=36\), \(\bar x=12.6\), \(s=3.0\) for \(H_0:\mu=12\) versus \(H_1:\mu\ne12\). They write: "Because \(n\) is large, use \(z=(12.6-12)/3=0.20\). The P-value is \(P(Z>0.20)=0.4207\), so there is no evidence." Identify the errors and give the appropriate large-sample conclusion.
solution_text: The denominator is wrong: the standard error is \(s/\sqrt n=3/6=0.5\), not \(s=3\). The test is two-sided, so the P-value should use both tails. Also, since \(\sigma\) is unknown, the formal statistic is T with \(df=35\), though for \(n=36\) the Z approximation is close. The correct statistic is \[t=\frac{12.6-12}{3/\sqrt{36}}=\frac{0.6}{0.5}=1.20.\] The two-sided P-value is approximately \(2P(T_{35}\ge1.20)\), about \(0.24\). Thus do not reject \(H_0\) at common levels such as 5%, but the student's arithmetic and P-value construction were still incorrect.
thought_text: A wrong solution can accidentally reach the same final decision. On exams, the reasoning and statistic still matter.
